turns.text: Hello Catrice, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s… it’s been a lot. Honestly, looking out at the fog here in Barnstable, I feel like I’m living in a version of my own life that I don’t quite recognize. 

You’d think being a pediatric nurse would make this transition easier, right? That’s what everyone keeps telling me. "Catrice, you’re a pro, you’ve seen it all." But that’s exactly the problem. Because I *have* seen it all. I’ve seen the things that go wrong in the NICU, the things that aren't supposed to happen. Now that I’m home with my daughter, I can't just switch that off. When she sleeps, I don't rest; I watch her chest rise and fall, counting breaths, waiting for a pause that I’m terrified will last too long. 

The care I received… it was technically perfect. My midwives were skilled, everything went by the book. But there was this distance. They saw the nurse, not the woman who was shaking on the inside. Every time they told me I "knew what to do," I felt like they were pushing me further away. I don't want to be the expert right now. I just want to be a mom. I want someone to look at me and ask how *I* am doing, not how I’m managing the clinical tasks. It’s lonely, being the one who’s supposed to have all the answers.
Tell me about the people who surrounded you in the days and weeks after your baby was born. Who showed up for you, and who were you surprised by — either by their presence or their absence?

It’s funny, the people I thought would be my rock—my colleagues at the hospital—have been the ones I’ve had to distance myself from the most. When they visit, they walk in with this assumption that because I’m one of them, I don’t need the "new mom" tutorial. They’ll make a joke about me being the only one in the room who knows how to handle a cord clamp, and they mean it as a compliment, but it feels like a heavy weight. It’s like they’re giving me a pass on needing support because they think I’ve got the technical side covered. But I’m not a nurse in my living room; I’m just me, and I’m scared.

My mom has been trying, but she’s older and she’s got her own way of doing things that sometimes clashes with my "textbook" brain, which causes its own tension. Honestly, I’ve been most surprised by a few friends from outside the medical field. They don't know the resuscitation protocols. They don't know what a healthy fontanelle feels like. They just hold my baby and tell me I’m doing a good job. They don't expect me to be a "pro." With them, I can actually let my shoulders drop for a minute. It’s the ones who *don’t* know how to do my job who actually make me feel the safest.

Tell me about the people involved in your feeding journey — whether you breastfed, formula-fed, or mixed-fed. Who helped you, who gave you conflicting advice, and how did those relationships shape your choices?

Feeding has been one of the most fraught parts of this whole experience. I felt so much pressure to "get it right" because, in my head, I knew the benefits of breastfeeding, the immunology, the bonding—I’ve lectured enough mothers on it myself. But when I actually sat down to nurse, it wasn't this magical, clinical success story. It was painful, and she was frustrated, and I felt like I was failing a test I’d written the curriculum for.

The hospital staff were supportive, but in a way that felt like they were reinforcing my professional identity rather than my maternal one. They’d say, "You know the latch technique better than anyone, Catrice." And I did! I knew the mechanics perfectly. But knowing the mechanics doesn't stop the exhaustion or the feeling that your body is being drained dry. 

When I brought up supplementing with formula, I felt like I had to justify it like a patient in a study, citing my fatigue levels and her weight gain patterns just to avoid judgment. I felt like I needed to be a "perfect" nurse-mom. I wish someone had just looked at me and said, "However she eats, she’s fed, and you’re doing enough." Instead, I felt like I was constantly defending my choices against the ghost of my own clinical training.

Tell me about the people who noticed — or who missed — changes in your emotional wellbeing after your baby was born. Who checked in with you about how you were feeling as a person, not just as a new mother?

That’s the thing—nobody really asked. At least, not in a way that invited an honest answer. They’d ask, "How’s the baby?" or "Is she sleeping?" and I’d give them the vitals, the output, the weight gain. I’m so good at reporting that stuff; it’s second nature. But when I’d try to hint at the fog, or the way the silence at 3:00 AM feels like a physical pressure on my chest, people just sort of glazed over or pivoted back to the baby.

I think they see my job title and assume I’m equipped to handle the emotional labor myself. Like I have some secret stash of resilience that the other moms don't have. But I don't. I’m twenty-three. I’m tired in a way that sleep doesn't fix. There was one midwife during a follow-up who lingered a second too long near the door. She looked at me—not at the chart, not at the baby—and asked, "Catrice, how are *you* holding up?" I almost started crying right there, but I panicked. I just said, "Oh, you know, just adjusting." I felt like if I admitted I wasn't okay, I’d be admitting I wasn't a "good nurse" or a "good mom." I’ve spent my life caring for everyone else; I don't think I know how to ask for that care in return.
Who else was involved in picking up on signs that you were struggling — a health visitor, your GP, your partner, a friend?

There really isn't a "partner" in the picture, so that’s a silence in the house that’s louder than you’d think. It’s just me and my daughter, which makes the loneliness feel absolute. 

As for a GP or health visitor? They check the boxes. They see a young, healthy woman with a professional background, a safe home, and a baby who is meeting all her milestones. From their perspective, I’m the "ideal" patient—someone who is low-risk, compliant, and clearly educated. They don't look at the eyes that haven't slept in three weeks or the way I physically recoil if someone suggests I "should" be enjoying this. They see the nurse, so they assume the mind is as controlled as the chart.

There was one friend, Sarah, who isn't in healthcare—she works in marketing. She’s the only one who didn't let me hide. She came over last week, looked at the pile of unwashed bottles and the way I was just staring at the wall, and she didn't ask about the baby’s feeding schedule. She just took the baby, put her in the bassinet, and told me to go take a shower. She sat outside the bathroom door and just talked about mindless, stupid things. She didn't treat me like a clinician; she treated me like a person who was drowning. That was the first time I felt like I could actually breathe.
Tell me about how your relationship with your partner — or your closest person — changed after your baby arrived. Who were you to each other, and how did the arrival of a baby reshape that?

Since I’m doing this on my own, the "closest person" to me has been my mom. Our relationship has always been a bit complicated—she’s the one who pushed me to get my nursing degree, she’s always been so proud of me being the "stable, successful one." But since the baby arrived, that dynamic has been really hard to navigate.

Before the baby, we were a team—she was my cheerleader, and I was the daughter who made her look good. Now, though, I feel like I’m constantly being judged by her standards of motherhood. She’ll watch me hover over the bassinet or check the baby’s vitals and tell me to "relax" or "stop being so neurotic." She doesn't understand that for me, this isn't just nerves—it's a learned trauma from the unit. I try to explain it to her, but she just sees me as "over-nursing" my own kid. 

It’s made me feel really isolated from her. I want to tell her that I’m struggling, that I’m grieving in a way I didn't expect, but I can't because she’s so invested in the idea that I’m the "pro." I feel like I have to keep performing this role of the perfect, capable nurse-mom for her, or else I’m letting her down. We’ve gone from being close to having this huge, silent wall between us.
How did that affect your sense of who you were — as a partner, as an individual, as a new parent — in those early weeks and months?

It’s like I’ve lost the map to myself. Before, my identity was so clear: I was the diligent student, the reliable nurse, the daughter who made everyone proud. I had a clear path. Now, all those pieces feel like they've been thrown into a blender. 

In these early weeks, I’ve felt like I’m constantly performing. When I’m at the clinic for follow-ups, I’m "Catrice the Pediatric Nurse," the woman who knows exactly what’s happening. Then I come home, and the mask slips, and there’s just this terrified, exhausted twenty-three-year-old who feels like she’s faking it. I don't feel like a "new parent" in the way the books describe; I feel like a monitor. My whole world has narrowed down to heart rates and breathing patterns. 

I’ve lost the part of me that just *is*. I don't listen to music anymore because I need to hear the baby’s breathing. I don't read for pleasure because I’m reading medical journals to see if I’ve missed some obscure sign of distress. I’ve become my own patient, in a way, and it’s exhausting. I feel like a ghost in my own house, just haunting the nursery, waiting for the other shoe to drop. I’m mourning the girl I was a few months ago—someone who could sleep through the night without checking on someone else’s life.

Tell me about the healthcare professionals who supported you after your birth — your community midwife, health visitor, GP. Which of those relationships felt continuous with your pregnancy care, and which felt like starting again from scratch?

The transitions between providers felt like being passed through a series of checkpoints, and honestly, none of them were connected. My midwife, who saw me through the pregnancy and delivery, felt like the only one who actually knew my baseline. But once the birth was over, that relationship just… evaporated. It was like I was signed off, and then I was suddenly in the hands of the health visitor, who felt like a complete stranger starting from page one.

It’s incredibly jarring to have to recount your entire birth story and your mental state to someone new who’s just looking at a stack of paperwork. I had to explain my nursing background to every single person I met, over and over, because they’d see the "No partner" and the "Pediatric nurse" labels and jump to their own conclusions. The GP felt the most detached. It was a ten-minute slot, very clinical, very "box-ticking." I wanted to tell them that the clinical, objective side of me is actually what’s hurting me, but the structure of those appointments doesn't allow for that. 

There was no continuity of care for *me* as a person. I felt like a set of vitals being handed off. It made me feel so small—like I wasn't a human being moving through a major life transition, but just another chart they needed to close.

Tell me about your return to work — or your decision not to return — and the people involved in that transition. Who supported you, who put pressure on you, and how did that shape your experience of becoming a working parent?

The thought of going back to the unit feels like standing on the edge of a cliff. I’m technically still on leave, but the emails from the hospital are starting to pile up, and the expectation is clear: they’re "saving my spot" because I’m a high-value nurse. My manager keeps checking in, framing it as "We really need your expertise back on the floor," which, again, feels like a compliment that’s actually a cage.

There’s this unspoken pressure that because I know the hospital system, I shouldn’t need the transition time that a "normal" mother gets. My mom is the one pushing the hardest for me to go back. She’s worried about the finances, obviously—doing this alone makes the money issue very real—but she also has this image of me as the person who never falters. She thinks getting back to work will "pull me out of this funk," like if I just get back to the IVs and the charts, I’ll magically stop feeling so fragile.

But I’m terrified. How am I supposed to walk onto the neonatal unit and care for someone else’s baby when I’m still struggling to trust that my own is safe in her crib? If I see a baby in distress, am I going to be able to compartmentalize, or am I going to shatter? I feel like I’m being forced back into a role that I’m not sure I can play anymore. I’m not the same person I was before the delivery, and I don't think they’re ready to see that.
Tell me about the communities — online or in person — that became part of your postpartum world. Who did you connect with, and what did those communities offer that more formal support couldn't?

To be honest, I haven't really been able to bring myself to join any of the "Mommy & Me" groups in Barnstable. I walked past one in the park the other day, and I just couldn't do it. I have this irrational fear that if I start talking to other moms, they’ll ask what I do for a living, and as soon as I say "pediatric nurse," the dynamic will shift. Either they’ll treat me like a walking medical dictionary and start asking me for free advice—which is the last thing I have the capacity for—or they’ll feel intimidated, and I’ll feel even more isolated.

I did find one anonymous forum online for parents who have experienced a loss, though I haven't posted much yet. Just reading their threads… that’s where I’ve felt the most seen. There’s no pressure to be the "expert" there. Everyone is just raw. Reading about their anxiety, their inability to sleep, the way they obsess over their babies' health—it’s the first time I’ve realized my "nurse brain" isn't a superpower that’s failing me; it’s a trauma response. 

Formal support is all about benchmarks: weight, feeding, development. These online spaces are about the *ghosts*. They offer a place where I don't have to be the professional. I can be the girl who is scared of the dark, and nobody tells me to "get it together" because they know exactly how heavy that darkness is. It’s the only place where I feel like I’m allowed to just be a broken, worried mother.
Tell me about any moments after your birth when you felt that you — as a person, not just as a new mother — were seen and cared for. Who made that happen, and what did they do or say?

There’s one moment that stands out, and it’s not from a doctor or a midwife. It’s from a neighbor of mine, Mrs. Gable. She’s an older woman, quiet, and she’s never once asked me about the baby’s feeding chart or her growth percentiles.

A couple of weeks ago, it was raining, the kind of cold, grey rain we get here off the Atlantic. I was standing on my porch with the baby in the carrier, just trying to feel a breeze that didn't smell like sterile wipes and laundry detergent. I was crying—just quiet, shaking tears—and Mrs. Gable walked over. She didn't offer advice. She didn't tell me to sleep when the baby sleeps, and she didn't ask me to explain why I was upset.

She just looked at me and said, "Catrice, you look like you’re holding up the whole world, and you’re allowed to put it down for a second."

She made me a pot of tea and sat on the porch with me for an hour. We didn't talk about nursing or medicine or the stress of my job. She talked about the local history, or the tides, just boring, steady, beautiful things. She treated me like a person who was tired, not a nurse who was "adjusting." In that hour, I wasn't the expert; I was just a woman being looked after. It was the first time I felt like it was okay to be soft.
Who else was involved in moments of genuine care for you — not just the baby — that stayed with you?

There was a lactation consultant—not the ones at the hospital, but someone I reached out to privately after I’d been home for a few weeks. I was dreading the appointment because I figured she’d just be another person assessing my technique or judging my supplemental feeding, but she was different.

She walked in, looked at the chaos of my living room—the stacks of burp cloths, the half-empty water bottles, the pile of medical journals I hadn't opened in days—and she didn't comment on the clutter. She just sat down on the floor next to me and said, "You look like you've been running a marathon in your own head."

She didn't start with the latch or the position. She started by asking me what I missed about my life before the baby. It sounds like such a simple thing, but nobody had asked me that since I was pregnant. They all assumed my life *was* the baby now. When I told her I missed the quiet of my walks along the coast, she insisted I go for a twenty-minute walk while she held the baby. She promised—and she sounded like she really meant it—that she wouldn't call me if anything happened, that she would handle it.

I walked down to the water, and for those twenty minutes, I didn't count a single breath. I didn't check my pulse. I was just Catrice, walking in the wind. When I got back, she didn't give me a report on the baby’s performance; she just handed her to me and smiled. It was the first time I felt like someone saw the person underneath the scrubs. That hour saved me, I think. It gave me permission to exist outside of the nursery walls.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, there’s one thing that keeps coming back to me, especially when the sun goes down here in Barnstable. I think the thing people—the healthcare system, my colleagues, even my mom—don't quite grasp is that being "low risk" on a chart doesn't mean you’re "low risk" in your head. 

I’ve spent my shifts assessing patients with such clinical focus, but there’s a massive blind spot in the way we care for providers who become patients. We act like the professional training acts as a buffer against the raw, terrifying reality of postpartum life. It doesn't. If anything, it’s a magnifying glass. Every potential complication I’ve ever seen in a shift is suddenly a shadow in my nursery. 

I just want the people designing these maternity systems to understand that if a woman walks into your clinic with a stethoscope in her bag, she’s not necessarily "the expert" in that room. She’s just a woman who is likely terrified and over-analyzing everything because she knows too much. Stop asking us what we think the protocols should be and start asking us how we’re holding up in the quiet. Just once, I want a provider to look at me, put the chart down, and say, "You don't have to be a nurse today. You can just be you." That’s all. It sounds small, but for me, it would have changed everything.
